did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically YOLOv8 for mole detection and EfficientNetB0 for binary classification (benign vs. malignant), to analyze facial images for early melanoma detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including the design of a web-based intelligent framework (Smart Mole) using YOLOv8 and EfficientNetB0 for melanoma detection. It details a specific methodology involving data acquisition from ISIC and Kaggle datasets, image preprocessing, model training (30 epochs), and evaluation on a test set of 200 images, reporting new performance metrics such as 99.3% accuracy. Although published in conference proceedings, it contains original results and technical implementation details rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Web-based Intelligent Framework for Early Facial Melanoma Detection using Deep Learning
journal: Proceedings of the 3rd International Conference on Intelligent Cyber Physical Systems and Internet of Things (ICoICI-2025)
brief_summary: This study presents Smart Mole, a web-based intelligent framework for the early detection of facial melanoma using standard smartphone or webcam images. The system employs YOLOv8 for mole localization and EfficientNetB0 for binary classification of lesions as benign or malignant. The model was trained on a custom dataset of 1,600 labeled facial mole images sourced from the ISIC archive and Kaggle, split into training, validation, and test sets. Evaluation on a test set of 200 images demonstrated an overall accuracy of 99.3%, with precision and recall scores exceeding 93%. The lightweight architecture allows for real-time inference at approximately 25 frames per second on mobile devices, facilitating accessible, non-clinical skin cancer screening.

lesion_types_studied.details: Binary classification target; labeled as class 1 in the dataset (Yi ∈ {0 (benign), 1 (melanoma)}). The system classifies facial moles as malignant (melanoma) or benign.

Binary classification comparator; labeled as class 0 in the dataset. The paper explicitly states the limitation of 'binary classification without considering other lesion types' and focuses only on distinguishing benign moles from melanoma.

all_task_types.details: YOLOv8 is used to identify and localize moles in facial images, providing bounding boxes and Mean IoU (mIoU) of 0.86.

EfficientNetB0 performs binary classification of detected moles into benign (class 0) or malignant/melanoma (class 1) categories using a SoftMax layer and a threshold of 0.5. The model achieved 99.3% accuracy, 94.1% precision, and 93.7% recall on a test set of 200 images.

datasets_used.name: Custom Facial Mole Dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset of 1,600 high-resolution facial mole images sourced from the ISIC archive and Kaggle (specifically the skin-cancer-mnist-ham10000 dataset). The images were manually labeled by experts as either benign or malignant based on clinical criteria. The dataset was split into training, validation, and test sets.

use.training: Used to train the EfficientNetB0 classification model and YOLOv8 detection model.
use.validation: Used to fine-tune hyperparameters and assess model generalization.
use.testing: Used to evaluate final results on unseen data to validate real-world performance.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To derive and calculate classification performance metrics including Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-score.
To eliminate mean bias and stabilize model training by normalizing pixel values.
To center data and reduce variance during training.
To make binary classification decisions (melanoma vs. benign) based on predicted probability.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) derived from a confusion matrix on a test set.

Custom Facial Mole Dataset (Sourced from.notes: Tested on non-clinical selfie images. Minimal false positives and false negatives confirmed high reliability. Usability rating from user testing: 9.3/10.

Dermoscopic Clinical Images (Reference C.notes: Table II lists performance on dermoscopic clinical images for comparison purposes. The paper states Smart Mole exceeds these values while working with real-world, non-dermoscopic selfie images.

architecture_used.main_model: Smart Mole (YOLOv8 for detection + EfficientNetB0 for classification)
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning used for EfficientNetB0; specific pretraining dataset not explicitly named for EfficientNetB0 in the text, but YOLOv8 is described as 'fine-tuned'.
architecture_used.input_size: Not specified (described as 'high-resolution face images' and 'cropped mole patches').

architecture_details.detection_model: YOLOv8n (YOLOv8 nano variant for lightweight performance)
architecture_details.feature_extraction: EfficientNetB0 encoder extracts feature vector F from cropped mole regions detected by YOLOv8

training_details.optimizer: Not specified (CLEO optimizer mentioned in context of related work [14], not explicitly for this model's training)

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer followed by SoftMax

preprocessing_techniques: Z-score normalization to eliminate mean bias and stabilize model training
Pixel normalization (A_norm = (A - μ) / σ) to center data and reduce variance
Cropping of detected mole regions (patches) using YOLOv8 for focused analysis
Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) mentioned in related work context for enhancing local contrast
conclusions: The proposed Smart Mole framework, utilizing YOLOv8 for detection and EfficientNetB0 for classification, achieves high clinical-grade accuracy (99.3%) and performance (Precision: 94.1%, Recall: 93.7%, F1 Score: 93.9%) on non-clinical, real-world facial selfie images.

The system demonstrates superior generalizability compared to existing models trained on dermoscopic clinical images, effectively handling the challenges of everyday, non-controlled imaging conditions.

The lightweight architecture enables real-time inference on mobile devices (approximately 25 FPS on Snapdragon 865) and desktops (over 40 FPS on NVIDIA RTX 3060), facilitating accessible, mobile-friendly screening without dependence on high-end computing hardware.

The framework successfully bridges the gap in public health tools by integrating detection, classification, and melanoma awareness education into a single, user-friendly web-based platform deployable via Streamlit and ONNX.

The study acknowledges limitations including the binary classification scope (benign vs. malignant only), the lack of data augmentation techniques, and the need for future work to address hair presence and other external variances in patient groups.
important_remarks: The study is limited to binary classification (benign vs. malignant melanoma) and does not account for other skin lesion types.
The dataset is small (1,600 images total) and sourced from ISIC and Kaggle, which may not reflect global skin tone diversity.
The system is optimized specifically for facial moles and may not be valid for histopathological diagnosis or non-facial lesions.
Performance is sensitive to variations in lighting, low-quality images, occlusions (hair, accessories, makeup), and extreme angles.
No data augmentation techniques were used; the authors suggest GAN-based augmentation as future work to improve robustness.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; results are based on descriptive metrics.
Potential privacy concerns exist regarding the processing of facial images in a web-based deployment.
The model's performance on very low-end devices is limited, with inference optimized for mid-range to high-end hardware (e.g., Snapdragon 865, RTX 3060).
The system was tested on non-clinical selfie images, which introduces variability compared to controlled dermoscopic clinical images.
